skill.name: ghe-design
skill.description: Guidance for creating clear, domain-specific requirements documents with verifiable acceptance criteria and useful implementation context.
skill.summary: Helps agents write flexible requirements documents that fit the project domain.
skill.author: Emasoft

security_audit.summary: The skill is a single markdown guidance file with no executable scripts. Most static findings are false positives caused by markdown fences, inline code examples, and external reference links, but the skill does authorize writes to ~/.claude and requires full reports to be posted to GitHub issue threads. These instructions create moderate filesystem and disclosure risk in agent workflows.

medium_findings.title: Hidden Claude Configuration Write Permission
medium_findings.description: The skill permits background agents to write to ~/.claude for plugin and settings fixes. This is a hidden home-directory configuration path that can affect future agent behavior, so users should review any changes before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction explicitly names ~/.claude as an allowed write location. The file is guidance, not executable code, so impact depends on agent behavior.

medium_findings.title: Mandatory Full Report Posting May Disclose Sensitive Data
medium_findings.description: The skill requires all reports to be posted in full to a GitHub issue thread and to GHE_REPORTS. If a report contains private code, credentials, customer data, or internal findings, this rule could disclose sensitive information outside the local workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The posting requirement is explicit and applies to full report text. The risk is contextual because GitHub visibility and report contents are not shown in this file.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged many markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution. The reviewed lines are code fences, inline paths, or example document content, and no executable Ruby, shell script, or command invocation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are markdown delimiters and inline examples. No command runner, interpreter call, or script file exists in the reviewed skill.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to public documentation and reference material for requirements examples. The skill does not instruct the agent to fetch these URLs automatically or send data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear under References sections in example requirements. There is no evidence of automated network access or credential transmission.

low_findings.title: Browser Storage Alert Is An Example Requirement
low_findings.description: The localStorage reference appears in an acceptance checklist for a dark mode toggle example. It is not code that accesses browser storage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside a markdown example showing acceptance criteria. No browser script or storage access implementation is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography blocker alerts map to markdown text, frontmatter, headings, examples, or security terminology. No cryptographic implementation, deprecated algorithm call, or hashing routine is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file contains prose and requirements examples, not executable cryptographic code. Some cited lines do not contain cryptographic operations at all.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent Write Scope Includes Hidden Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: Allowing writes to ~/.claude can modify agent or plugin configuration outside the project directory. This should be treated as sensitive filesystem access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path is explicitly listed as permitted write scope. There is no direct malicious payload, but the permission is security-relevant.

dangerous_patterns.title: Reports Required Outside Local Workspace
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs agents to post full report text to GitHub issue threads. This can move sensitive project information to a remote collaboration system.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is clear, but actual exposure depends on the issue visibility and report contents.

content.value_statement: Teams need requirements that fit each domain without rigid templates. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to create clear, verifiable requirement documents.
actual_capabilities: Guides agents to write requirements in structures suited to the project domain.
Defines mandatory elements such as identifiers, purpose, references, and acceptance criteria.
Provides example requirement patterns for algorithms, games, finance, distributed systems, security, and UI work.
Encourages verifiable acceptance criteria instead of vague completion statements.
Separates permanent requirements documents from operational reports.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not generate requirements by itself.
It assumes a GHE workflow with named agents such as Athena and Hephaestus.
It includes reporting rules that may not fit private or regulated projects.
It does not validate requirements against real project constraints automatically.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Feature
use_cases.description: Create a concise requirements document with scope, rationale, references, and testable acceptance criteria.

use_cases.title: Specify Technical Architecture
use_cases.description: Describe distributed systems, security boundaries, or data flows in a format engineers can implement.

use_cases.title: Prepare Implementation Handoff
use_cases.description: Turn user intent into a clear document that another agent or developer can build from.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Basic Requirements
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ghe-design to draft a requirements document for this feature: [feature]. Include what, why, acceptance criteria, and references.

prompt_templates.title: Add Domain Detail
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a specialized domain.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ghe-design patterns to write requirements for [domain]. Choose the structure that fits the domain and include edge cases.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Engineer Handoff
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a requirements handoff for [project]. Include implementation context, failure modes, verification steps, and open questions.

prompt_templates.title: Review Existing Requirements
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced quality review of a draft document.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this requirements draft using ghe-design. Identify missing mandatory elements, unclear acceptance criteria, and domain-specific gaps: [draft].

output_examples.input: Draft requirements for a dark mode toggle.
output_examples.output: A requirements document with a clear feature description, user value, persistence expectations, system preference behavior, transition requirements, and acceptance checks.
A security-focused requirements document covering threats, trust boundaries, authentication flow, token behavior, required headers, and verification criteria.
A gap review that checks compliance needs, transaction flow clarity, refund constraints, audit logging, and references to payment provider documentation.

best_practices: Use the structure that best matches the domain instead of forcing a fixed template.
Make every acceptance criterion observable, testable, and tied to user value.
Confirm before posting full reports to remote issue threads or shared locations.
anti_patterns: Do not treat the example sections as mandatory for every project.
Do not replace precise acceptance criteria with broad phrases like working correctly.
Do not include sensitive data in reports that will be posted to external systems.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps agents write clear requirements documents that fit the project domain and include verifiable acceptance criteria.

faq.question: Is this a strict requirements template?
faq.answer: No. It says templates are references and that the document structure should match the domain.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it run code or install dependencies?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed skill is a markdown guidance file and does not include executable scripts.

faq.question: What security caution should users know?
faq.answer: It permits writes to ~/.claude and requires full reports to be posted to GitHub issue threads.

faq.question: When is this skill most useful?
faq.answer: Use it before implementation when user intent must become precise requirements for developers or other agents.
